China's economy is facing new pressures from the US-Iran war, with manufacturing hubs like Foshan experiencing reduced factory orders, increased costs, and job losses. While China weathered Trump's tariffs last year, the Middle East conflict is now taking a toll on its export-driven economy.

triadic_ontology.findings: Mark Pelley's "The Relational Ground: A Transcendental Metaphysics from First Principles" (April 2026) develops a systematic metaphysics from the primitive that being is irreducibly relational. Through transcendental analysis, Pelley demonstrates that only non-coercive, self-giving relationality (agápē) satisfies structural conditions for determinate existence, requiring real triadic plurality as the minimal sufficient condition. This triune agápēic ground yields pure actuality, free creation, creaturely freedom, and the modal coherence of incarnation. Meanwhile, the TRIERTIUM Research Group advances a new Trinitarian ontology rooted in Czech pansophy tradition, as Matteo Raffaelli discusses in Modern Theology (February 2026). Inspired by Jan Amos Comenius's triadic synthesis of sciences and arts, this framework explores relational thinking grounded in Trinitarian participation, bridging philosophy, theology, and arts through transdisciplinary projects. Verlag Karl Alber's new series " [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional UAP transparency efforts intensified this week as the Pentagon released its first tranche of 162 declassified UFO files Friday, following President Trump's February order for full disclosure. The documents span decades and include FBI, State Department, and NASA materials. Representative Anna Paulina Luna's Task Force on Declassification continues pressing for withheld evidence, having requested 46 additional UAP videos that whistleblowers claim AARO already possesses. In classified SCIF briefings, senior senators including Marco Rubio have reportedly received testimony about recovered non-human craft and biological entities, with former congressional staffer Kirk McConnell confirming these accounts mirror decades-old classified reports. The House Oversight Committee held hearings featuring military whistleblowers describing encounters with craft demonstrating physics-defying capabilities, including MQ-9 drone footage showing a Hellfire missile bouncing harmlessly off an  [TRUNCATED]

weather: Today in Edmonds: Mostly sunny with some cloudy intervals. High 63-66°F, low tonight 48-51°F. Very low rain chance (1-10%). Winds NNW at 5-15 mph. Evening will be partly cloudy with light north winds.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council has rescheduled its vote on Stephanie Lucash's appointment as city administrator for Tuesday, May 12. The council postponed the original May 5 vote to allow more time for consideration of this "high-level and high-profile position." If confirmed, Lucash, currently Kenmore's deputy city manager, would receive a $226,068 annual base salary for 2026. Separately, the council advanced a proposal to "unpause" the volunteer Edmonds Salary Commission to its May 12 consent agenda, which would make salary recommendations for the mayor and councilmembers for the 2027-2028 biennium.

The City of Edmonds issued an update on ongoing efforts to resolve operational issues at the Edmonds Wastewater Treatment Plant. The $31 million carbon recovery system, which has experienced significant delays and technical failures since construction began in 2021, recently underwent a multi-day trial run at the end of April 2026. Jacobs Engineering, hired as an independent consultant for $415,000, is still evaluating the results. While some elements appeared encouraging, other issues remain concerning and require further review. The city has spent over $5 million hauling wastewater solids to an Oregon landfill due to the system's failures.

An armed carjacking in Woodinville led to a multi-county chase and several car crashes on Sunday, according to the Snohomish County Sheriff's Office. The incident began in Woodinville and involved pursuit through multiple jurisdictions, though specific details about injuries or arrests were not immediately available.

A DUI suspect was arrested after fleeing the scene of a fiery crash in north Lynnwood. The incident occurred just after 6:00 a.m. Sunday when witnesses reported a vehicle crash and fire. The suspect attempted to leave the scene but was taken into custody by authorities.

classroom_ready.title: Token Economy Implementation Guide – 10th Grade ELA
classroom_ready.summary: Four-step turnkey protocol: (1) pinpoint target behavior, (2) choose token type (chips, punch-cards, play money), (3) co-design reinforcer menu with students, (4) set spending rules. Emphasizes pairing every token with specific praise and fading tokens once the behavior is mastered. Includes reinforcer ideas that cost nothing (first-in-line pass, errand-runner privilege, special chair).

classroom_ready.title: Hero Economy Launch – Printable Classroom Kit
classroom_ready.summary: Complete starter kit: Hero Bank Tracker worksheet, Prize Power Menu poster, Reward Pass coupons, and Master Ledger. Uses non-tangible rewards (extra computer time, homework pass, lunch with teacher) to reinforce positive behavior. Ready-to-print PDFs and editable templates included.

classroom_ready.title: Starting Your Own Token Economy – Full Lesson Plan
classroom_ready.summary: Walks teachers through launching a token economy in one prep period. Includes student-facing slide deck, behavior-contract template, reinforcer-menu worksheet, and troubleshooting FAQ. Stresses starting with 3–5 target behaviors and letting students help price the rewards to boost buy-in.

classroom_ready.title: How to Create a Classroom Economy
classroom_ready.summary: Step-by-step setup: pick daily target behaviors, design ‘classroom cash’ with school mascot, build a visible rewards chart, and run a weekly class store. Provides printable $1/$5/$10 bills and 20 no-cost reward coupons (free time, sit-with-friend pass, etc.). Focus on positive reinforcement and real-world money skills.

pedagogy_pieces.title: Reddit Thread: First-Year ELA Teachers Swap Management Hacks
pedagogy_pieces.summary: Crowd-sourced thread where veteran ELA teachers share quick wins: daily 5-minute journal prompts as bell-ringers, color-coded late-work bins, and a ‘two-minute tidy’ routine that cuts transition chaos. Multiple users vouch for the silent-signal system (hand raise = voices off) and praise the simplicity of a single weekly participation grade instead of daily points.

english_lesson_plans.notes: All resources are free and classroom-ready; the four token-economy guides give Lang a menu from quick-start (LiveSchool) to full semester economy (Talkin’ Town). The Reddit thread adds low-prep management tricks vetted by current ELA teachers.

intellectual_journals.findings: Jan-Werner Müller's 'Short Cuts: Orbán's Fall' traces the poetic justice of Viktor Orbán's political demise after sixteen years of 'illiberal' rule. The essay details how Orbán pioneered autocratic legalism—using proper legal procedures to entrench executive power—while creating a Christian nationalist ideology centered on natalism and anti-globalism. His regime became a laboratory for far-right populism, copied by figures like Poland's Kaczyński and Trumpists, but ultimately collapsed when Fidesz won less than 39% of the vote in April 2026. Müller examines how Orbán's dependence on German ind [TRUNCATED]

James Butler's 'Labour's Failure' analyzes the catastrophic collapse of the Labour Party in England's May 7 local elections, where voters delivered a national referendum on Keir Starmer's leadership rather than local governance. The essay argues that Labour's defeat reflects not just policy failures but the death of intergenerational party loyalty in a multi-party democracy still operating under duopoly-era structures. Butler depicts a Britain where young people face declining life expectancy, crushing debt, and diminished prospects, making anger a rational response to a 'rudderless and compro [TRUNCATED]

In 'Do octopus brains work like humans'—or is there another way to be smart?', neuroscientist Liam Drew explores how octopuses and other cephalopods evolved entirely different neural architectures for intelligence. Despite 600 million years of separate evolution from vertebrates, cephalopods developed large, complex brains with convergent features like camera eyes and advanced cognition. The article examines how these soft-bodied mollusks use their distributed nervous systems—where two-thirds of neurons reside in their arms—to demonstrate tool use, problem-solving, and delayed gratification, o [TRUNCATED]

The Spring 2026 issue features Tanvi Misra's essay 'The Death of Asylum,' which traces how centuries of efforts to deny refuge to persecuted people paved the way for contemporary authoritarianism. The piece examines the historical evolution of asylum law from ancient religious traditions through modern nation-state systems, arguing that the gradual erosion of refugee protections reflects broader transformations in sovereignty and human rights. Misra connects historical patterns of exclusion—from medieval sanctuary laws to 20th-century refugee regimes—to current anti-immigration policies, demon [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Is the Twenty-fifth Amendment Really an Option?
newyorker.dek: After J.F.K.’s assassination, a neophyte lawyer named John Feerick was summoned to Washington to draft the provision. Now everyone wants him to weigh in on booting Trump from office. Diego Lasarte writes.
newyorker.text:
John Feerick, the eighty-nine-year-old law professor who, in 1964, wrote the bulk of the Twenty-fifth Amendment, is used to people asking him questions about the niceties of replacing a sitting President. Mostly, he dodges them. The other day, in his tidy office at Fordham Law School, he talked about the amendment’s concept of “inability,” in terms of a President’s fitness for office—but steered clear of Donald Trump. “You cannot remove powers and duties from a President without the Vice-President agreeing,” Feerick said, speaking generally. He has a full head of white hair, and wore a gray suit and orthopedic sneakers.
Feerick’s signature creation, the Twenty-fifth Amendment, has been in the news lately, most recently when President Trump was hustled out of the White House Correspondents’ dinner, after an intruder showed up with alleged plans to murder the President and top members of the Cabinet. Naturally, questions arose about the line of succession. If the gunman had been successful, Chuck Grassley, the ninety-two-year-old president pro tempore of the Senate, who skipped the dinner, would have been sworn in as President. “Half the Cabinet at least was there,” the House Speaker, Mike Johnson, who is second in line and was present at the event, said. “They’re going to have to reëvaluate this.”
Section 1 of the Twenty-fifth Amendment states that “In case of the removal of the President from office or of his death or resignation, the Vice President shall become President.” (Curiously, J. D. Vance was rushed off the stage before Trump was.)
Feerick’s involvement began in 1963, when he was a newly minted graduate of Fordham Law with a job at Skadden Arps, handling small-claims-court cases. He published an article in the Fordham Law Review about Presidential succession, an issue that had come up during the Eisenhower Administration, when the President weathered several grave illnesses. (“Eisenhower really wasn’t able to function for a good while,” Feerick said.) “I sent the article to President Kennedy, I sent it to Robert Kennedy, to Ted Kennedy,” he said. He also sent it to Arthur Krock, the venerated Times columnist, who responded with an admiring note, saying, “I wish Congress were as much interested as you and I.”
Weeks later, John F. Kennedy was assassinated, and Krock devoted his next column to Feerick’s argument. (“Clem!” Feerick called to his assistant, asking her to find the clip, amid shelves containing books he has written. “This one—‘From Failing Hands: The Story of Presidential Succession’—is out of print,” he said.)
Feerick was heartsick; he’d seen J.F.K. and his wife in a ticker-tape parade in New York. Soon, the brand-new President, Lyndon Johnson, “took an interest in the subject,” Feerick said. The article, which Feerick’s wife, Emalie, had helped with, went viral, or as viral as something could go in the sixties. Feerick was summoned to Washington to start writing what would become the Twenty-fifth. “I had to ask the law firm if they would pay me to stay overnight and take a train or plane down there,” he remembered. “I mean, I had no money. My parents were immigrants.”
Feerick spent a lot of time in D.C. One day, he said, “I got there late because the planes weren’t running. And I walked right up to the White House from the gate! I opened the door of the White House. Which I guess would be impossible today.”
Among the twelve people enlisted to work on the amendment, Feerick was the expert on Presidential vacancies. “There were eight times when Presidents died in office and Vice-Presidents succeeded,” he said. Half of these deaths were assassinations—Lincoln, Garfield, McKinley, and J.F.K. (Lincoln’s son Robert was either present at or near the first three deaths. After McKinley’s, Robert declined invitations to Presidential functions, saying that a “certain fatality” followed him.)
Feerick is also an expert in Presidential incapacity. “In conversations about drafting an amendment during Eisenhower, they talked about the ‘insanity’ of the President,” he said. Section 4 of the amendment states that the Vice-President and a majority of the Cabinet can boot a President if they determine him to be “unable to discharge the powers and duties of his office.” Before the Twenty-fifth, Presidents had informally handed over power to the Vice-President during medical issues or temporary absences, but always voluntarily. “An insane President would probably disagree with such a determination,” Feerick said.
Last month, Trump tweeted that, if Iran did not agree to his demands, “a whole civilization will die,” prompting several calls to oust him using the Twenty-fifth. Representative Jamie Raskin introduced a bill that would create a bipartisan commission of physicians, psychiatrists, and retired government officials, which could act alongside the Cabinet in the case of the amendment being invoked.
Feerick, asked to consider whether Trump’s unhinged posts, one of which included an A.I. image of himself as Jesus, might substitute for a medical diagnosis, opted to take a long view. “There are Presidents galore from the country’s history that you can find issues with their mental health,” he said. He brought up Richard Nixon. After the Watergate scandal broke, “Nixon was having a lot of difficulty, according to reports I read, and drinking and so forth.” Senior White House staff discussed invoking the Twenty-fifth as Nixon spiralled into paranoia. But Nixon resigned, sending Gerald Ford to the Oval Office.
Feerick took pains to explain that Section 4 is not designed to be a tool used by an opposition party to remove a President. The amendment, he said, was born out of logistical concerns, such as the possibility of a President becoming “unconscious for any of a number of reasons and out of reach during a crisis,” or “if his plane went down.” After Ronald Reagan was shot, Alexander Haig, the Secretary of State, ran into the press room and declared, “I am in control here in the White House!,” even though the Twenty-fifth had not been invoked, and he was actually fourth in line.
Despite the section’s wonkily procedural intentions, it is still regularly mentioned by Trump’s opponents, and Feerick is often contacted by reporters for his expertise. “I’m not getting involved in what the Vice-President should do,” he said. During Joe Biden’s Administration, when the Twenty-fifth came up in debates about whether a President can be too old, Feerick did speak up about one vital point. He remembers saying, at a panel, “Age is individual-specific.” ♦

enriched_articles.title: 'Make up their own minds': Pentagon releases first tranche of UFO files
enriched_articles.text: The United States Department of Defense has released its first tranche of previously classified files on unidentified flying objects (UFOs), following an order from US President Donald Trump in February. The release on Friday contained 162 files dating back decades, including documents from the FBI, the Department of State, and the US space agency, NASA. It was accompanied by the launch of a new, heavily stylised website to house the documents, using white typewriter-style font against a black b

enriched_articles.title: Rubio and Senators Heard 'Credible' SCIF Testimony on Non-Human Beings and Recovered Alien Bodies
enriched_articles.text: Senior US senators including Marco Rubio have been told in classified settings that highly intelligent non-human beings may be real, according to a former top congressional staffer. The claim, delivered publicly by Kirk McConnell, suggests that lawmakers heard detailed testimony inside secure facilities about recovered alien craft and even non-human bodies. Kirk McConnell is not a fringe figure. He served for 37 years across the Senate Armed Services Committee, the Senate Intelligence Committee
